query: delayed tooth eruption in congenital hyperthyroidism

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Clinical Significance -- Delayed Eruption
retrieved_docs.content: When the timing of tooth emergence significantly deviates from the established norms associated with ethnicity and gender, it is referred to as delayed eruption. [2] Delayed eruption can result from various causes, including local factors such as mucosal barriers, gingival fibromatosis, odontogenic and nonodontogenic tumors, premature loss of a primary tooth, and radiation damage. Moreover, delayed eruption can be associated with systemic conditions and endocrine disorders such as hypothyroidism, [19] hypopituitarism, hypoparathyroidism, and certain medications. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Clinical Significance -- Premature Eruption
retrieved_docs.content: The premature eruption of teeth can be attributed to various factors, including genetic abnormalities, disruptions in endocrine function involving the pituitary, thyroid, and gonads, congenital syphilis, local factors such as excessive resorption of the overlying bone, and environmental factors such as exposure to pollutants, including toxic polyhalogenated aromatic hydrocarbons, polychlorinated biphenyls, polychlorinated dibenzodioxins, and dibenzofurans. In addition, poor maternal health and episodes of fever during pregnancy can also contribute to premature tooth eruption.

retrieved_docs.content: Pituitary dwarfism Hypopituitarism Growth hormone-related defects

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Clinical Significance -- Delayed Eruption
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic and prolonged malnutrition, often associated with vitamin deficiencies during childhood, is another factor that also correlates with delayed eruption. [2] Moreover, various syndromes have been identified as causing delayed eruption, including Down syndrome, Gardner syndrome, cleidocranial dysostosis, anhidrotic ectodermal dysplasia, Hutchinson-Gilford syndrome, Bloch–Sulzberger syndrome, Apert syndrome, and Axenfeld–Rieger syndrome. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Pendred Syndrome -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: A thyroid goiter may appear in late childhood or early adulthood. Most people have normal thyroid levels, but hypothyroidism can happen.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Number -- Differential Diagnosis -- Hyperdontia
retrieved_docs.content: For patients with hyperdontia, the following differential diagnoses should be considered: Genetic syndromes, eg, cleidocranial dysplasia, Gardener syndrome, or trichorhinophalangeal syndrome Variation of normal anatomy

retrieved_docs.title: Cockayne Syndrome -- History and Physical -- Dental Abnormalities
retrieved_docs.content: The deciduous teeth may appear late and exhibit characteristics such as being small, irregular, and abnormally spaced. In certain instances, permanent teeth may not develop; when they do, they remain small or poorly implanted. Children affected by this condition are more prone to cavities than the average population.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Structure -- Differential Diagnosis -- Dens Evaginatus/Talon Cusp
retrieved_docs.content: Gemination Fusion Enamel hyperplasia

retrieved_docs.title: Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Dental pathologies are commonly observed in these patients, such as hypodontia of permanent teeth, delayed eruption, and dentin dysplasia. [20] A lack of attached gingiva could be a pathognomonic feature. [21] Thus, dentists have a crucial role in early diagnosis and management. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Diseases of the Eyelashes -- Etiology -- Hypotrichosis
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperthyroidism may cause ciliary madarosis

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Number -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Supernumerary teeth, if malpositioned, can prevent the normal eruption of developing teeth, which would require removal, a potentially invasive procedure. The multiple supernumerary teeth are generally indicative of an underlying syndromic or developmental condition, which the appropriate clinician should evaluate.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Clinical Significance -- Natal and Neonatal Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: Certain syndromes have been reported to be associated with the occurrence of natal and neonatal teeth. These syndromes include chondroectodermal dysplasia, Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome, Pierre Robin syndrome, neonatal progeria, cleft lip or palate, ectodermal dysplasia, craniofacial dysostosis, and Down syndrome.

retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis for each anomalous condition is outlined below. Additional diagnostic tools, such as radiographic evaluation and genetic testing, may aid in distinguishing between these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In forensic dentistry, the eruption is critical for diagnosing specific growth disturbances and estimating a child's chronological age when their birth records are unknown. [15] Moreover, comprehending the eruption schedule is crucial for proactively managing occlusion, particularly when deciding the ideal timing for deciduous tooth extractions and initiating orthodontic treatment. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Shape and Size -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Microdontia is a rare dental anomaly characterized by teeth that appear smaller than expected. True generalized microdontia, a condition affecting all teeth, often occurs in individuals with systemic syndromes, such as pituitary dwarfism and orofaciodigital or oculomandibulofacial syndrome. [1] [2] [3] Younger patients who have undergone chemoradiotherapy may also develop microdontia due to disruptions in dental development. [4] True generalized microdontia rarely occurs in individuals without underlying genetic or developmental conditions.

This category includes renal disorder during pregnancy, peripheral neuropathy during pregnancy, and polymorphic eruption of pregnancy, each representing a separate organ‑system complication that can arise in the context of gestation and is classified under the broader label of other specified pregnancy‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2688
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic factors, indicated by the allowed qualifier, are recognized as relevant contributors to several distinct medical conditions. Specifically, a genetic component is associated with التسمم الوشيقي (viral intoxication), اِضطِراب اللسان (tongue disorder), فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ (hyperthyroidism), and قرحة قلاعية (aphthous ulcer). The presence of a genetic predisposition suggests that hereditary influences may play a role in the development or susceptibility to each of these disorders, underscoring the importance of considering family history and genetic screening in their assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2074
retrieved_docs.content: Antithyroid preparations are classified as a type of diiodotyrosine, a compound that participates in thyroid hormone synthesis, and they are employed to inhibit thyroid hormone production in the management of hyperthyroid conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2281
retrieved_docs.content: Mild hyperemesis gravidarum, thyroid dysfunction during pregnancy, and gestational herpes are all specific maternal complications that fall under the broader, uncategorized group of pregnancy complications affecting the mother. Each of these conditions represents a distinct health issue that can arise during gestation and requires appropriate clinical attention despite not being classified within more specific subcategories of maternal pregnancy disorders. Recognizing them as part of the general category of unclassified maternal pregnancy complications helps clinicians consider a wider range of potential problems when evaluating and managing pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.content: planning, diagnostic work‑up, and parasitological evaluation to a wide spectrum of congenital, metabolic, infectious, endocrine, renal, ophthalmic, reproductive, and neurological conditions, thereby supporting comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1843
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation are classified under the term تقييد النمو الجنيني, which denotes fetal growth restriction. This condition is understood to be part of a broader, unspecified disorder that originates in the perinatal period, referred to as حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة. Recognizing that تقييد النمو الجنيني falls within this larger perinatal context helps clinicians assess and manage infants presenting with these growth‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1828
retrieved_docs.content: The conditions نوبة (seizure), خلل الحركة المتأخر (late‑onset movement disorder), فطار غزوي, intestinal infection, and إجهاض تلقائي غير كامل (incomplete spontaneous abortion) are all recognized as entities that can be qualified as chemically induced. Identifying a chemical trigger as a possible cause is important for the evaluation and management of these diverse clinical presentations, because exposure to certain substances may precipitate seizures, movement abnormalities, gastrointestinal infections, and incomplete abortions. Awareness of the chemically induced nature of these conditions helps clinicians consider appropriate diagnostic testing, preventive strategies, and targeted therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 325
retrieved_docs.content: Antithyroid preparations are pharmacologic agents that counteract excess thyroid hormone production and are employed in the treatment of hyperthyroidism (فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ). By inhibiting the synthesis of thyroid hormones, these medications help normalize elevated metabolic activity, alleviate symptoms such as tachycardia, weight loss, and tremor, and reduce the risk of complications associated with uncontrolled hyperthyroidism. Effective use of antithyroid preparations is therefore a central component of managing patients with فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2759
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotherapy is regarded as an allowable therapeutic option in several specific clinical contexts. It is permitted for patients presenting with وضعية المَجيء المقعدي, for individuals who have thyroid disorders, and in situations of تهديد بالإجهاض (threatened abortion). This indicates that radiotherapy is not contraindicated in these particular conditions and may be considered appropriate when clinical judgment deems it necessary.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), indicating that children born prematurely with low birth weight have an increased risk of developing reduced height later in life.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized as a related group of adverse perinatal outcomes and are classified together as a distinct clinical category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2802
retrieved_docs.content: Alzheimer‑type dementia, glandular disease, and hyperthyroidism are all conditions that can be influenced by genetic factors. Genetic predisposition is recognized as an important contributor to the development of Alzheimer‑type dementia, affecting the risk and progression of this neurodegenerative disorder. Similarly, certain glandular diseases have hereditary components that may affect gland function and pathology. Excess thyroid activity, or hyperthyroidism, also exhibits a genetic basis, with inherited variations influencing susceptibility to thyroid hormone overproduction. Understanding the genetic underpinnings of these disorders aids in risk assessment, early detection, and the development of personalized therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1443
retrieved_docs.content: Antithyroid preparations are a class of medications that inhibit thyroid hormone synthesis, and they encompass specific agents such as diiodotyrosine and carbimazole. Both diiodotyrosine and carbimazole function as antithyroid drugs, sharing the common therapeutic purpose of reducing excessive thyroid hormone production in conditions like hyperthyroidism. Recognizing that these compounds belong to the broader category of antithyroid preparations helps clinicians select appropriate treatment options and understand their pharmacologic relationships.
